content.problemStatement:
The education system of Wonderland was designed by the great psychologist Math Hatter. In Wonderland there are **n** kids attending school. For simplicity they are numbered from **1** to **n**. 
Math Hatter noticed that all of them are very smart, so he measured their Intellectual Quotient (IQ), it turned out that no two students had the same IQ and that all IQs were integers in the range from **1** to **n**.


In the Math Hatter's education system, one kid can be the teacher of many other kids, and also be the pupil of many others.

Let **j** be the smallest integer greater than **i** such that the IQ of kid **j** is greater than the IQ of kid **i**, the pupils of kid **i** are all the kids numbered from **i + 1** to **j - 1**. If no such **j** exist we consider **j** to be equal to **n + 1**, note that kid number **i** will not have any pupils when **j = i + 1** or when **i = n**.


Math hatter doesn't remember the IQ of each kid, however he remembers their number of pupils. Using this information Math Hatter is trying to recover the IQ of each kid. Help him calculating the number of IQ assignations that he can find. As the answer could be large, output it modulo **109 + 7**.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains one integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains one integer n, the number of kids, the second line contains n space separated integers ci denoting the number of pupils of kid i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the answer to the problem modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there are three possible assignments of IQs: [1, 4, 3, 2], [2, 4, 3, 1], and [3, 4, 2, 1]. For the second test case, there are no possible assignments because the last student cannot have one pupil.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to calculate the number of ways to assign IQs to the kids. We start by initializing a DP array where dp[i] represents the number of ways to assign IQs to the first i kids. We then iterate over each kid and update the DP array based on the number of pupils for each kid. We use modular arithmetic to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the number of kids in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — where n is the number of kids in each test case.
